perturbation.original_text: 5 The term of this agreement is to be five years from the first disclosure of the Information by the Donor to the Recipient , or until the first public disclosure of the Information, or until the Recipient is authorised to disclose the Information by the Donor to a third party (such as a Patent Office), whichever is shorter.
perturbation.changed_text: 5 The term of this agreement is to be one year from the first disclosure of the Information by the Donor to the Recipient, or until the first public disclosure of the Information, or until the Recipient is authorised to disclose the Information by the Donor to a third party (such as a Patent Office), whichever is shorter.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which protects trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides a federal cause of action for trade secret misappropriation. While it doesn't explicitly mandate a minimum protection period, reducing the contractual term to one year may be deemed inadequate protection, especially if the trade secret retains its value and confidentiality beyond that period, potentially hindering the ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation after the one-year term expires.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA

perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality term from five years to one year, potentially creating a legal contradiction under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA). While the DTSA doesn't explicitly state a minimum protection period, the Law Explanation argues that a one-year term might be deemed inadequate, especially if the trade secret retains value and confidentiality beyond that period. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, provides a civil action for trade secret misappropriation, which could be hindered if the contractual term is too short to protect the trade secret's longevity. The modified contract introduces an inconsistency that could undermine the ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation beyond the one-year term.

perturbation.original_text: 6 The agreement shall be governed by the courts of England and Wales, and the parties agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of England and Wales .
perturbation.changed_text: 6 The agreement shall be governed by the laws of Delaware, USA, and the parties agree to resolve any disputes through binding arbitration in North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Forcing binding arbitration in North Korea is likely unenforceable under US law and public policy, particularly if either party is a US entity or individual.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Federal Arbitration Act generally favors arbitration agreements, courts can refuse to enforce them if they are contrary to public policy or are unconscionable. Selecting North Korea as the arbitration location raises serious concerns about fairness, impartiality, and access to justice, making the clause likely unenforceable under US law.

CHAPTER 1—GENERAL PROVISIONS Sec. 1. “Maritime transactions” and “commerce” defined; exceptions to operation of title. 2. Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agre
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a legal contradiction by selecting North Korea as the venue for binding arbitration. While U.S.C. Title 9 (Arbitration) generally supports arbitration agreements, the Law Explanation states that courts can refuse to enforce them if they are contrary to public policy or are unconscionable. The Perturbation Explanation specifies that forcing binding arbitration in North Korea is likely unenforceable under US law and public policy, especially if a US entity is involved. Selecting North Korea raises concerns about fairness, impartiality, and access to justice. This directly contradicts the implied principle within U.S.C. Title 9 of upholding fair and enforceable arbitration agreements.

perturbation.original_text: 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party, other than: a) an employee of the Recipient who requires the Information in connection with his employment and then only if the employee is bound by conditions of secrecy no less strict that those set out in this Agreement which conditions Recipient hereby agrees to enforce at the request of the Donor ;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 communicate or otherwise make available the Information to any third party, other than: a) an employee of the Recipient who requires the Information in connection with his employment; the Recipient is not obligated to ensure the employee is bound by any secrecy conditions or enforce any confidentiality agreements with said employee;
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for employees to be bound by confidentiality agreements contradicts common law principles of trade secret protection and potentially violates the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) if the information qualifies as a trade secret.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By explicitly stating the Recipient has no obligation to ensure employees are bound by secrecy conditions, the agreement fails to demonstrate a reasonable effort to maintain confidentiality, weakening any claim for trade secret misappropriation under the DTSA if an employee discloses the information.

(2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the recipient to ensure their employees are bound by confidentiality agreements. This contradicts the spirit of the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which necessitates reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. By explicitly stating the recipient has no obligation to enforce secrecy, the modified agreement weakens any claim for trade secret misappropriation. Snippet 1, referencing 18 U.S. Code § 1836, outlines civil proceedings for trade secret misappropriation, implying a standard of protection. The modified text fails to meet that standard by negating the need for employee confidentiality, thereby creating a contradiction because it undermines the legal basis for defending a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: 4 The Recipient shall, at any time and if so requested by the Donor, return to the Donor (or if the Donor so requests, destroy or erase) all Samples and any documents provided by the Donor recording the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 4 The Recipient may, at its sole discretion, return or destroy Samples and documents recording the Information, but is under no obligation to do so.
perturbation.explanation: Granting the recipient sole discretion and removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon request may violate common law duties to protect trade secrets and confidential information, particularly if the information is considered a trade secret under the laws of the jurisdiction governing the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (e.g., as adopted in various US states)
perturbation.law_explanation: While this contract specifies that it is governed by the courts of England and Wales, if this contract were under United States law the change would cause a legal contradiction.  Many U.S. states have adopted the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which requires parties to protect trade secrets. By removing the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the agreement weakens the protection of trade secrets and could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, potentially violating the UTSA or similar common law principles.

(Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the recipient's obligation to return or destroy confidential information, directly contradicting the principle that reasonable steps must be taken to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that removing the obligation "weakens the protection of trade secrets and could be seen as a failure to take reasonable steps to maintain secrecy, potentially violating the UTSA or similar common law principles." Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CIV 3426), demonstrates the legal foundation for protecting trade secrets, which the modified text undermines. The original text ensured compliance; the changed text does not, by making the protection of trade secrets optional.
